transcript.presenting_concern: Trauma narrative processing — domestic violence exposure

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Hey Brianna. Come on in. How are you today?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I'm okay. Tired. We had testing at school this week so it's been a lot of sitting and concentrating and I'm over it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: State testing? That's exhausting. How'd you feel about how you did?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: English was fine. Math was... not my best. But whatever. It's done.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Fair enough. Testing is stressful for everyone. How was the rest of your week? How are things at your foster mom's house?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Good actually. Ms. Denise made this rule that Fridays are movie night and I get to pick the movie. Last Friday we watched this old movie she wanted me to see — "The Wizard of Oz." I'd never seen it. It was weird but kind of good? The flying monkeys were creepy though.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: [laughs] Those flying monkeys are terrifying — you're not wrong. That sounds like a really nice tradition. Having predictable, positive routines in a home — that helps build safety. Speaking of which — how's the sleep been?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Better. My foster mom said I slept through the night three times this week, which — I mean, that might not sound like a lot to normal people but for me it's a record. I've been having nightmares basically every night since... since everything. So three full nights without waking up screaming? That's huge.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Three full nights IS huge, Brianna. That's real progress. What do you think is helping?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Probably the relaxation stuff we practiced. I do the muscle thing — tense and release — before bed every night now. Like, I start with my feet, squeeze them for five seconds, let go. Then my legs. Then my stomach. All the way up. By the time I get to my face I'm already half asleep. And I keep the nightlight on like you said. The one that's just this soft purple glow — not bright, just enough that if I wake up I can see the room and know where I am. It's less scary when it's not pitch dark. When it's dark I wake up and for a second I don't know where I am and I think I'm back... there.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That makes complete sense. When you wake in total darkness, your brain can't immediately confirm "I'm safe, I'm in my room at Ms. Denise's." So for a few disorienting seconds, your brain defaults to the last time you felt scared in the dark — which was at home, when things were happening. The nightlight gives your brain immediate visual confirmation: "This is my room. I'm safe." It bypasses that disoriented fear response.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. Exactly. Like, purple glow equals safe. Dark equals... maybe not safe. My brain just needs that one second of "oh okay, I'm here" and then I can go back to sleep.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's your nervous system building a new association. Purple light equals safety. Before, your nighttime associations were all linked to danger — darkness meant listening for yelling, listening for the sound of doors, never knowing what would happen. Now you're building new nighttime associations: muscle relaxation, soft light, quiet house. Your brain is re-learning that nighttime can be peaceful.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I never thought about it that way. Like I'm teaching my brain something new.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's exactly what you're doing. And the progressive muscle relaxation — the tension-and-release — works because it gives your body a physical signal that it's safe. When we're scared, our muscles tighten automatically. It's the body's armor. When you deliberately tighten and then release, you're telling your body "there's no threat here. You can let go of the armor. You don't need protection right now."

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I like thinking of it as armor. Like, my body used to have to wear armor to bed every night because it didn't know what was coming. And now it's learning to take the armor off.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's a beautiful way to put it. You're very insightful, you know that? Okay. So — last session we started working on your trauma narrative. Your story. We got through the first chapter — life before things got bad. You told me about when it was you, your mom, and Jaylen, and things were okay. Today I'd like to continue into the next chapter if you're feeling up to it. But only if you're ready. There's no pressure. We can always spend today on something else if it doesn't feel right.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [pause] Yeah. I've been thinking about it all week actually. I know I need to get through this part. It's like... the stuff I haven't said out loud yet is sitting in my chest getting heavier. And talking about it last time — even though it was scary — afterward I felt lighter. Like I'd set down a backpack I'd been carrying.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's such an important observation. The trauma memories, when they're held inside and never spoken, they tend to stay stuck — frozen in time, with all the original intensity. When we tell the story out loud, in a safe space, the memories get processed. They move from "frozen" to "past." They become something that happened to you rather than something that's still happening inside you. But I want to remind you of our safety tools before we start. Remember — you're telling the story. The story isn't happening to you right now. You're safe in this room. If it gets too intense and you need a break, we use our signal — thumbs down — and we stop immediately. No questions asked. We can do grounding, we can take a walk, we can talk about something else. You're in control of this process. Okay?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay. I remember. Thumbs down for stop. And the breathing thing if I start feeling floaty.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: The square breathing — breathe in for four, hold for four, out for four, hold for four. That keeps you anchored here if you start to feel like you're drifting away or going back into the memory too deeply. You can also press your feet into the floor and feel the ground — that's an anchor too. Ready?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Ready.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Okay. So last time you told me about life before — when it was you, your mom, and your little brother Jaylen. You described your apartment, your school, your mom's laugh. Things were good until your mom started dating Marcus. You mentioned they met at her job. That's where we stopped. What happened next?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: At first Marcus was nice. Actually, he was really nice. Charming, I guess is the word. He brought flowers for my mom. He brought me a drawing tablet because he knew I liked art. He brought Jaylen a basketball. He took us all to the park — like, actual family outings. I remember one Saturday he took us to the zoo and bought us everything — cotton candy, the stuffed animals from the gift shop. My mom was happy and when she was happy, everything felt normal. Like we were a normal family doing normal things. I wanted a dad. I never had one and Marcus felt like... maybe this could be it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So there was hope. He was filling a role that had been empty. How old were you then?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Ten. Almost eleven. Jaylen was seven.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And how long was it nice like that? The zoo trips and presents?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Maybe... three or four months? Long enough that he moved in. Long enough that I started calling him Marcus instead of "my mom's boyfriend." Long enough that I trusted him. And then... he changed. Or maybe — I think about this a lot — maybe he didn't change. Maybe he just showed who he really was. Like the nice stuff was a costume and once he was living there, once my mom was attached, he didn't need the costume anymore.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's a very mature insight. What you're describing is actually a well-documented pattern in domestic violence — it's called the "honeymoon phase" or "love bombing." The abuser presents their best self to establish the relationship and build attachment. Once the attachment is secure — once the other person is invested — the controlling and abusive behaviors emerge. Your mom wasn't foolish for trusting him. He was strategic about gaining trust first. That's what abusers do.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: My mom blamed herself. For letting him in. For not seeing it coming. But how could she? He was so good at pretending.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: She couldn't have known. No one could. The mask was convincing because it was designed to be. What did you start noticing when things shifted?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The yelling. That was the first sign. He'd yell at my mom over little things — dinner wasn't ready when he got home, the house was messy, she looked at her phone too long when he was talking, she laughed too loud with her friends, she wore something he didn't like. Everything she did was wrong suddenly. And the yelling wasn't normal arguing — it was mean. He'd call her names. "Stupid." "Worthless." Say she was lucky anyone wanted her. Say no one else would put up with her and her two kids.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: How did your mom respond when he said those things?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: She got smaller. Like physically smaller. Her shoulders would come in, her voice got quiet, she'd apologize even when she hadn't done anything wrong. She stopped laughing. She stopped playing music in the kitchen while she cooked — she used to always have music on. The apartment went quiet. And she started flinching when he walked into the room. Like her body was bracing for something before he even spoke.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You noticed your mom's fear before anyone told you what was happening. Kids are incredibly perceptive about changes in their parents — especially emotional changes. You read the room because you needed to. That hyper-awareness of other people's emotional states — that was you learning to track danger. How did all of this make YOU feel?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Scared. Confused. Because during the day at school everything was normal and then I'd come home and it was like entering a different world. I couldn't talk about it to anyone because I didn't even know what to call it. He wasn't hitting anyone yet. He was just... mean. And my teacher would ask "how's everything at home" and I'd say "fine" because technically nothing was happening. Just yelling. Everyone's parents yell, right? I thought maybe I was overreacting.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You weren't overreacting. What you're describing — the verbal abuse, the name-calling, the controlling behavior, your mom's fear response — that's domestic violence even without physical hitting. Emotional and verbal abuse IS abuse. Your body knew it was dangerous even if your mind didn't have the words for it. How old were you when it escalated?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I'd just turned eleven. Jaylen was eight. The yelling had been going on maybe two months when... it got physical. I remember the first time because I'd gone to bed and I heard — [voice gets quiet] — I heard this sound. Like... something hitting something. And then my mom crying. Not her normal frustrated crying. Like, pain crying. Scared crying.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Take a breath if you need to, Brianna. You're doing great. You're here with me.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [deep breath] I'm okay. So... I was in bed. And I heard it. And I knew what it was even though I'd never heard it before. My body just... knew. And I wanted to go help. I wanted to run out there and — I don't know — do something. Yell at him. Call someone. Get between them. But I was frozen. My body wouldn't move. Like I was glued to the mattress. I couldn't even lift my arms. And I just lay there listening to my mom cry and I couldn't. Do. Anything.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: [gentle voice] Brianna, what you're describing — that frozen feeling — I want you to understand something really important about it. Can I teach you about what was happening in your body that night?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: When our brains detect a serious threat — real danger — our nervous system has three main responses. You've probably heard of fight or flight. Fight means confronting the danger. Flight means running from it. But there's a third one that most people don't talk about: freeze. Freeze is when the nervous system assesses the situation and determines that fighting is too dangerous — the threat is bigger and stronger — and running isn't possible — there's no safe escape route. So instead, the body goes still. It's like playing dead. It's an ancient survival response — animals do it too. A rabbit in headlights. An opossum playing dead. It's not choosing to do nothing. It's the nervous system making a split-second decision to protect you by keeping you still and invisible.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: But it felt like I was choosing. Like I was being a coward.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: It wasn't a choice. Let me say that clearly: the freeze response is involuntary. You did not choose to stay in bed. Your nervous system chose FOR you, in a fraction of a second, based on its assessment of the danger. And its assessment was correct. You were an eleven-year-old child. Marcus was an adult man who was already being violent. If you had gotten between them, what might have happened?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: He could have hurt me too.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Yes. Your nervous system knew that before your conscious mind did. The freeze response is intelligent — it's not weakness. It kept you safe. It kept you invisible. It kept you alive and unharmed that night. That's not cowardice — that's survival.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: But I should have called 911. I had a phone. It was right there on my nightstand. I could have reached for it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: How old were you?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Eleven.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Eleven years old. In bed. In the dark. Hearing your mother being hurt by a man who terrified you. With your body in full freeze mode — which means your limbs literally felt paralyzed. Even if you'd WANTED to call, your motor system was offline. That's what freeze does — it shuts down voluntary movement. You physically could not have reached for that phone any more than you could have flown out the window. This is brain science, Brianna. Not character. Not bravery versus cowardice. Biology.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [quiet, slightly tearful] I hear what you're saying. And part of me believes it. But part of me still thinks... I should have done something. Anything.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And that's the part we're going to keep working on. That "should have" is what we call a stuck point — a belief that formed during the trauma and hasn't been updated since. It made sense at age eleven to blame yourself because blaming yourself gives you an illusion of control: "If I could have done something different, then maybe I have power in this situation." Blaming the situation — accepting that you were truly helpless — is actually scarier. Because helplessness means you had no control. And for a child, that's terrifying to accept.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: So I blamed myself because that felt less scary than admitting I had no power?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Exactly. Self-blame is sometimes a child's way of maintaining a sense of agency in an overwhelming situation. "If it was my fault, then I can prevent it next time by being different." But the truth is: it was never your responsibility. The responsibility belonged entirely to Marcus — the adult who chose violence — and to the other adults around you who could have intervened. Not to an eleven-year-old child.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [wiping eyes] Can I tell you the rest? I want to keep going.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Of course. Take your time.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: After that first night... it got worse. More hitting. Not every day but enough that you never knew when it was coming. Like, some days Marcus would be normal — watching TV, eating dinner, even joking around — and I'd think "maybe it was a one-time thing, maybe it's over." And then out of nowhere he'd explode again. That was almost worse than if it was constant. Because you couldn't predict it. You couldn't prepare.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: The unpredictability is a huge factor in how traumatic an experience is. When danger is constant, the body at least adapts to a state of chronic alertness. But when danger is unpredictable — sometimes safe, sometimes terrifying, and no way to tell which is coming — the nervous system stays in a constant state of hyper-vigilance. Always scanning, always bracing, never able to relax. That's exhausting for an adult. For a child, it's devastating.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: That's exactly what it was. I was always scanning. Always watching his face when he came home. Trying to read his mood before he even spoke. Was he relaxed? Were his fists clenched? Did he look at my mom with that cold look? I got really good at reading people. Too good. I still do it. Like, with every adult, I'm trying to figure out if they're safe before they've even said anything.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That hyper-vigilance — the constant scanning of other people's moods — was an adaptive response to an unsafe environment. It helped you predict when things might escalate. But now that you're in a safe environment, the scanning continues even though you don't need it. It's like a fire alarm that's still going off after the fire is out. Part of our work together is helping your alarm system learn that it can stand down now. You're safe at Ms. Denise's. You're safe at school. You're safe here. The scanning can relax.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I notice myself doing it with Ms. Denise. She'll come home from work and I watch her face immediately. Is she stressed? Is she angry? Even though she's NEVER been mean to me. Never even raised her voice. But my body goes on alert anyway.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And that's not weird or broken — that's your body doing what it learned to do to survive. The good news is: it can unlearn it. Every day that Ms. Denise comes home and is safe and predictable and kind, your nervous system is collecting evidence. It's learning: "This adult is safe. I don't need the alarm with this person." Over time, the scanning will decrease. Not all at once. But gradually.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay. [pause] So — after that first night. Mom started wearing long sleeves. Even when it was hot. She put concealer on her face — I know what concealer looks like because I watch makeup videos. She was covering bruises. She told me and Jaylen that we couldn't tell anyone about what happens at home. That if anyone found out, they'd take us away from her. That we'd go to strangers. That we might get separated. So I kept the secret. For a year.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: A year. That's an enormous burden for a child to carry. How did the secret feel?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Heavy. Like this brick in my stomach all the time. I'd be at school laughing with my friends and then suddenly remember: "This isn't real. My normal life isn't real. At home there's this terrible thing and no one knows." I felt like a liar all the time. Like I was performing "normal kid" during the day and then going home to... that.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: The dual reality — one face for school, one reality at home — that's incredibly isolating. You were carrying an adult-sized secret with no support, no one to talk to, no one to help you process what you were witnessing. And your mom asked you to carry it because she was terrified — she believed the threat that you'd be taken away.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: And she was right. That IS what happened. They DID take us.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Yes. And we're going to get to that part. But first — during that year, what did you do when things were happening? When you heard the violence?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I took care of Jaylen. That was my job — or, I made it my job. No one told me to. But someone had to. So when the yelling started, I'd get Jaylen from wherever he was — the living room, the kitchen — and bring him to our room. I'd close the door. Put headphones on him. Play games on my phone with him. Make him laugh if I could. Talk about anything else — school, Minecraft, basketball. Try to fill his ears with anything other than what was happening outside our door. I thought if I could keep HIM from hearing it, maybe it wasn't really happening. Like, as long as one of us was protected, it wasn't as bad.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Brianna. You took on a protective, caregiving role at eleven years old that should never have been yours to carry. You were a child protecting a younger child because no adult was protecting either of you. That's called "parentification" — when a child takes on adult responsibilities because the adults in the situation can't fulfill them. And while it shows incredible love and strength on your part, it also means you never got to be just a kid yourself. You were always on duty. Always responsible. Always putting Jaylen first.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I mean... who else was going to do it? Mom couldn't. She was... [voice cracks] She was dealing with enough.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I know. And your compassion for your mom is beautiful. But I want to hold space for something: you deserved protection too. Not just Jaylen. You were also a child in that house. You were also hearing things no child should hear. And while you were putting headphones on Jaylen, no one was putting headphones on you.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [crying quietly] No one protected me.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: [soft voice] No. No one did. And that wasn't fair. And it wasn't your fault that you needed protection and didn't get it. You were a child. You deserved safety. You deserved adults who kept you safe. And the adults in your life at that time — including your mom, who loved you but was being abused herself — couldn't provide that safety. That's a loss, Brianna. A real loss. And you're allowed to grieve it.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [crying] I love my mom. I don't want to blame her.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You can love your mom AND acknowledge that you needed something she couldn't give you. Those aren't contradictions. Your mom was a victim too. She was being controlled, intimidated, physically hurt. Her capacity to protect you was diminished by her own trauma. That doesn't make her a bad mother — it makes her a person in an impossible situation. But it also doesn't mean you have to pretend you weren't hurt by the lack of protection. Both truths exist at the same time.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [sniffling] Okay. Both truths. I can hold both.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You can. Take a breath. Do you want to continue or do you want to pause?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [deep breath] I want to continue. I want to get to the end of it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Okay. What happened at the end?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The end came because of Jaylen. Marcus had never touched us — me or Jaylen. He hit my mom but he never hit us. I think that was my mom's line — "he doesn't hurt the kids" — which was how she justified staying. Like, "if he only hurts me, I can take it, the kids are safe." But then... one night. Jaylen was in the kitchen. He had a glass of juice and he tripped — he was eight, he's clumsy, kids trip — and the juice went all over Marcus's laptop. Marcus's expensive laptop that was open on the table. And Marcus... he backhanded Jaylen across the face. Hard. Split his lip. Blood on the floor. Jaylen screaming.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Where were you?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: In my room. I heard Jaylen scream — not his normal scream but a pain scream — and I ran out. And I saw the blood on his mouth and Marcus standing over him and my mom — my mom moved faster than I've ever seen her move. She was between them in a second. She grabbed a knife from the counter — a big kitchen knife — and she pointed it at Marcus and she said, her voice was shaking but she said, "Get out. Get out right now or I will kill you." And I believed her. In that moment I believed she would do it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What happened then?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: He left. He called her names on the way out. Called her crazy. Said he'd be back and she'd be sorry. But he left. And my mom dropped the knife and she was shaking and crying and she held Jaylen and she kept saying "I'm sorry, I'm sorry, I'm sorry." And then she looked at me standing in the doorway and she said — [voice breaks] — she said "Come here, baby" and I went to her and she held both of us and said "It's over. It's done."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What was that moment like for you?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Relief. Like, explosive relief. But also terror because he said he'd come back. And also anger — like, it took Jaylen getting hit for her to do something? I was angry at her for that. Even though I know it's not fair. I was angry that she let it go on for a year and only stopped it when Jaylen bled. Like... would my blood have been enough? Or did she need to see HIS blood specifically?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That anger makes sense, Brianna. You're asking: "What about me? Was I enough to protect?" And underneath that anger is hurt — deep hurt — that a year of witnessing wasn't enough to trigger the exit, but one act against Jaylen was. I want to be careful here because your mom's response is complicated. Abusers use psychological control — your mom may have genuinely believed she couldn't leave, that it would be worse if she tried, that she was protecting you by staying. Her breaking point came when the "rule" — he doesn't hurt the kids — was broken. That doesn't mean your pain didn't matter to her. It means she was trapped and couldn't see a way out until the trap changed shape.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I know that. In my head I know that. But my heart still says... why wasn't I enough?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And that's a feeling we're going to honor and work through. It's a valid hurt. You don't have to rationalize it away or make it smaller. It's okay to feel angry at someone you love. It's okay to feel hurt by someone who was also hurting. Your feelings don't have to be fair or logical — they just have to be felt and acknowledged.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [quiet] Okay. So... after that night. Mom called the police. They came and took pictures — of Jaylen's face, of my mom's bruises. They asked me questions. I was terrified because my mom always said if people found out, they'd take us away. And she was right. CPS came the next day. Two women with clipboards. They looked around our apartment. They talked to us separately. They saw Jaylen's lip. They saw my mom's bruises — the fresh ones and the faded ones. And then they said... [voice shaking] they said my mom "failed to protect" us. That she knew he was violent and kept us in the house with him. That the environment was unsafe. And they took us.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What happened in that moment when they said they were removing you?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [crying] My mom screamed. Like — not anger screaming. Like her heart was being ripped out. She grabbed Jaylen and she begged. She was on the floor begging them not to take us. She said she'd kicked him out, she said she'd do anything, she said she'd move, she'd get a restraining order, she'd do whatever they wanted. And they said it was "for the children's safety" and they had to follow protocol. And I was standing there watching my mom dissolve and I felt... nothing. I couldn't feel anything. It was like someone turned off my emotions. Everything went quiet and gray.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That numbness you're describing — that's another trauma response. Emotional shutdown. When the pain is too overwhelming for the system to process, it just... turns off. Like a circuit breaker tripping. Your body was protecting you again — same as the freeze, just a different form. Too much input, too much pain, system shutdown.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I didn't cry until that night. They took us to a temporary home — a stranger's house — and they put us in a room with bunk beds and Jaylen was crying and I was holding him and telling him it would be okay and I was fine, I was totally fine, I was in mom-mode — and then he fell asleep. And I was alone. And that's when it all hit me and I cried all night. I think that was the worst night of my life. Worse than the nights I heard Marcus hitting my mom. Because at least on those nights I was in my own bed in my own home with my brother. That night I was in a stranger's bed in a stranger's home and my mom was somewhere else crying alone.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: [gently] Brianna, I want to pause and check in with you. You're telling me incredibly painful things right now. How are you doing? In your body, right now. What do you notice?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: My chest is tight. My eyes are burning. My hands are squeezing — I didn't realize but I'm grabbing the pillow really hard. But... I'm here. I'm not frozen. I'm not numb. I'm here. I'm talking. I'm feeling it and it hurts but it's not... taking me over like before.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's the difference, Brianna. Before, in previous weeks, when these memories came up — what happened?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I'd shut down. Go numb. Couldn't talk. Sometimes I'd get dizzy and feel like I was floating above my body.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And right now?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Right now I'm feeling pain BUT I'm present. I'm here in your office. I know I'm safe. The story is in the past and I'm in the present telling it. It hurts but it's like... a clean hurt? Not the stuck-in-my-chest-can't-breathe hurt from before.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: "A clean hurt." I love that distinction. I think what you're describing is grief. Genuine, present-moment grief — mourning what happened to your family. And grief, while painful, is HEALTHY. It means you're processing the experience rather than being trapped in it. When you were numb and shut down, the pain was stuck. When you feel it and express it — like right now, with tears and tight chest and burning eyes — the pain is moving through you. It has somewhere to go.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Like the armor metaphor. The armor keeps the pain in too. Not just keeping danger out.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Exactly. When we armor up and shut down, nothing gets in — but nothing gets out either. The pain stays trapped inside the armor. Letting yourself feel it — in a safe space, with support — lets it flow through and out.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Dr. Huang? They punished my mom. That's what it feels like. She was being beaten for a year and when she finally fought back, when she FINALLY got him out — they punished her. They took her kids. How is that justice?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's a really painful injustice to sit with. And your anger about it is completely valid. The system's response — "failure to protect" — is a legal framework that doesn't account for the complexity of domestic violence. It doesn't account for the fact that leaving is the most dangerous time for DV victims. It doesn't account for the psychological control, the fear, the isolation that makes leaving feel impossible. Your mom was a victim being treated like a perpetrator. And for you and Jaylen, it felt like you were being punished for being victims too.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: It still feels that way. I'm not with my mom because the system decided she was the problem. Not Marcus — HER. He got arrested eventually but she's the one who lost her kids.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I hear that. And I want to acknowledge: you can hold compassion for your mom AND recognize that the separation, painful as it is, meant you were in a physically safe environment. Both things can be true. You needed safety. Your mom needed help. The WAY the system went about providing both of those things was traumatic in itself. That's a systemic failure that you're right to be angry about.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I miss her. So much. I talk to her Wednesday nights on the phone but it's not the same. Twenty minutes on the phone and I can hear her trying not to cry and I'm trying not to cry and we talk about stupid stuff — what I ate for dinner, how school is — because neither of us can talk about the real stuff on the phone with the caseworker listening.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Of course you miss her. She's your mom. And loving your mom and feeling angry about what happened — both at Marcus and at the system — those can all coexist. You're not required to pick one feeling. You can love her, miss her, be angry at the situation, and still be working toward healing. Do you know how her program is going?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: She's doing all the things they told her to do. Parenting classes. DV survivor group. Individual therapy. She got a new apartment. She got a restraining order against Marcus. Her caseworker says she's doing everything right. The review is in three months. Maybe... maybe we can go home.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's hopeful news. Your mom is working hard — just like you're working hard. Both of you healing on your own paths so you can come back together stronger. How does the possibility of going home feel?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Scary. Exciting. Scary-exciting. Like... what if it's different? What if she's different? What if I'M different? What if I can't be normal with her because every time I look at her I'll remember?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Those are all real questions and we'll work through them. Reunification is its own process with its own feelings. We don't have to solve it all today. What I want you to know is: you'll have support through that transition. From me, from your caseworker, from Ms. Denise. You won't be navigating it alone.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay. [deep breath] Can we stop the story part here for today? I feel like... I said a lot. More than I've ever said about it out loud to anyone.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Of course we can stop. You did incredible, incredibly brave work today, Brianna. You told the full arc of your story — from the beginning to the removal — out loud, with feeling, while staying present. That is HUGE progress. Six weeks ago you couldn't even say Marcus's name without shutting down.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. That feels like a long time ago now.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: It is. You've grown enormously. Let's do our closing grounding to make sure you leave here feeling solid and present. Okay? Feet on the floor. Feel the ground. Five things you can see?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [slower breathing] Your plant — the little succulent on your desk. The blue pillow I've been crushing. [laughs] Sorry about that. My shoes — the white ones with the stars. The clock on the wall — it's 3:42. And the painting of the ocean. The one with the waves.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Four things you can touch?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The couch fabric — it's soft and kind of bumpy. My bracelet — the one Jaylen made me at craft camp, the beaded one. My hair — I can feel my ponytail. And the tissue box — it's smooth and cool.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Three things you can hear?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The air conditioning — that humming sound. Something outside — a lawnmower? Somebody's mowing grass. And your voice. Talking to me.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Two things you can smell?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The lavender thing in here — is that a candle? And... I don't know, just regular air. Clean air.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: One thing you can taste?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The mint from my gum I chewed in the car.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. 5-4-3-2-1. You're here. You're in my office at 3:42 PM on a Thursday. The story is in the past — it happened but it's not happening now. You survived it. You're safe. And you're working through it. I'm really proud of you, Brianna. This was a hard session and you showed up for yourself.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: It did hurt. But you're right — it's the clean kind of hurt. The kind that means something is healing instead of just... festering.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Exactly. Like cleaning a wound — it stings but it's necessary for healing. The alternative is leaving it untreated and it just gets worse. For homework this week: continue the progressive muscle relaxation before bed. Keep the nightlight on. And — if you feel up to it — I'd like you to draw something from what we talked about today. It doesn't have to be realistic or pretty. It can be abstract — just shapes and colors that represent how you felt during the story. You're an artist — use that as a processing tool. Bring it in next week if you want to share it.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I can do that. I actually already kind of know what I'd draw. Like... a small figure in a dark room with a big bright shield around a smaller figure. That's me and Jaylen.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: The protector.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. [pause] And Dr. Huang? Thank you for not making this feel weird. Like, at school if someone found out about my "situation" they'd get all awkward and sorry-face and treat me like I'm fragile. You just... let me talk. You make it feel like it's okay to talk about it. Like I'm not broken for having this story.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You're not broken, Brianna. Not even a little. You're a kid who went through something terrible and came out the other side with empathy, insight, and courage. Your story doesn't define you — it's part of you, but it's not all of you. You're also the girl who picks the Friday movie. Who makes beaded bracelets with her brother. Who draws. Who sleeps through the night three times this week. You're so much more than what happened to you.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [quiet smile] Yeah. I am. Same time next week?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Same time next week. Take care of yourself. And Brianna — if the dreams come back or you feel overwhelmed between sessions, you have Ms. Denise's number and my crisis line number. Use them. You don't have to manage hard feelings alone anymore.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I know. I will. Bye, Dr. Huang.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Bye, Brianna. I'll see you next Thursday.

goals: Trauma narrative processing — domestic violence exposure
